text: Consider a `std::vector<int> vec;` and a loop adding 1000 elements. Which statements about its capacity and performance are true?

options.A: The vector's capacity will grow linearly, increasing by a fixed amount each time.
options.B: Reallocations can invalidate iterators pointing to elements within the vector.
options.C: Using `vec.reserve(1000)` before adding elements can improve performance by reducing reallocations.
options.D: After adding 1000 elements, `vec.capacity()` will always be exactly 1000.

text: You are implementing a singly linked list. Which code snippets correctly handle the deletion of a node `current` where `prev` is its preceding node?
code: struct Node { int data; Node* next; };

options.A: if (prev) { prev->next = current->next; delete current; } else { /* handle head deletion */ }
options.B: Node* temp = current; current = current->next; delete temp;
options.C: if (current->next) { current->data = current->next->data; Node* temp = current->next; current->next = current->next->next; delete temp; } else { /* handle tail deletion */ }
options.D: delete current; current = nullptr;

text: When choosing between `std::map<Key, Value>` and `std::unordered_map<Key, Value>` in C++, which considerations are generally true?

options.A: `std::map` offers guaranteed O(log N) complexity for insertions, deletions, and lookups.
options.B: `std::unordered_map` typically provides faster average-case O(1) operations but can degrade to O(N) in worst-case scenarios.
options.C: Keys in `std::map` must be hashable, while keys in `std::unordered_map` must be sortable.
options.D: `std::map` stores elements in a sorted order based on keys, while `std::unordered_map` does not guarantee any specific order.

text: Consider a `std::vector<int> v = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};`. Which operations can potentially invalidate iterators pointing to elements within `v`?

options.A: `v.push_back(6);`
options.B: `v.erase(v.begin() + 2);`
options.D: `v.clear();`

text: You need to store custom objects in a `std::set` based on a specific ordering. Which statements are true regarding custom comparators for `std::set`?

options.A: A custom comparison function or functor must define a strict weak ordering.
options.B: The comparator can be passed as a template argument to `std::set`.
options.C: The comparator must be a member function of the custom object class.
options.D: If `operator<` is overloaded for the custom object, a separate comparator is not needed.

text: Given a binary search tree, which traversal order would always print the elements in ascending order?

options.A: Pre-order traversal
options.B: In-order traversal
options.C: Post-order traversal
options.D: Level-order traversal

text: Regarding Breadth-First Search (BFS) and Depth-First Search (DFS) on a graph, which statements are true?

options.A: BFS uses a queue to explore vertices level by level.
options.B: DFS uses a stack (or recursion) to explore as far as possible along each branch before backtracking.
options.C: BFS is generally preferred for finding the shortest path in an unweighted graph.
options.D: DFS can detect cycles in a directed graph by checking for back edges.

text: Consider a simple custom linked list class. Which scenario is most likely to lead to a memory leak?
code: class LinkedList { Node* head; public: LinkedList() : head(nullptr) {} ~LinkedList() { /* destructor */ } void add(int val) { /* ... */ } };

options.A: The `add` method allocates a new `Node` but doesn't link it.
options.B: The destructor `~LinkedList()` only sets `head = nullptr;` without deallocating nodes.
options.C: A `Node` pointer is declared but not initialized.
options.D: The `add` method uses `new` but `delete` is called immediately after.

text: You are designing a binary tree node. Which smart pointer usage is appropriate for managing child nodes to prevent memory leaks and ensure proper ownership?
code: struct Node { int data; /* Smart pointer for left child */ /* Smart pointer for right child */ };

options.A: `std::unique_ptr<Node> left;` and `std::unique_ptr<Node> right;` for a standard tree.
options.B: `std::shared_ptr<Node> left;` and `std::shared_ptr<Node> right;` if multiple parents can share children.
options.C: `std::weak_ptr<Node> parent;` in conjunction with `std::shared_ptr` for children to break circular references.
options.D: Raw pointers `Node* left;` and `Node* right;` for simplicity, relying on manual deletion.

text: Analyze the time complexity of the following code snippet, assuming `N` is the size of `vec`.

options.A: O(N)
options.B: O(N log N)
options.D: O(N + M log M) where M is number of unique elements

text: Regarding `std::priority_queue` in C++, which statements are true?

options.A: By default, it implements a max-heap, meaning `top()` returns the largest element.
options.B: It can be customized to be a min-heap by providing a custom comparator (e.g., `std::greater<T>`).
options.C: Elements can be efficiently removed from the middle of the queue.
options.D: Its underlying container can be specified, with `std::vector` being the default.

text: Which of the following code snippets demonstrates a dangling pointer issue?

options.A: int* p = nullptr; *p = 5;
options.B: int* p = new int(10); delete p; *p = 20;
options.C: int x = 5; int* p = &x; x = 10;
options.D: int* p; p = new int[5]; delete[] p;

text: You need to sort a collection of custom objects. Which statements about sorting in C++ are true?

options.A: `std::sort` typically has an average time complexity of O(N log N).
options.B: For custom objects, `std::sort` requires an overloaded `operator<` or a custom comparison function/functor.
options.C: `std::stable_sort` is guaranteed to preserve the relative order of equivalent elements.
options.D: For small collections (e.g., N < 16), insertion sort is often faster than `std::sort`.

text: In `std::unordered_map`, what are common strategies for resolving hash collisions?

options.A: Linear probing, where the next available slot is sought.
options.B: Chaining, where a linked list stores multiple elements at the same hash index.
options.C: Quadratic probing, which uses a quadratic function to find the next slot.
options.D: Rehashing, which rebuilds the hash table with a larger number of buckets.

text: Which `std::deque` operations are suitable for implementing a queue (FIFO) data structure?

options.A: `push_back()` for enqueueing elements.
options.B: `pop_front()` for dequeueing elements.
options.C: `push_front()` for enqueueing elements.
options.D: `front()` to inspect the next element to be dequeued.

text: Consider a `const` member function in a data structure class. Which actions are generally permissible within such a function?
code: class MyList { int* data; size_t size; public: /* ... */ void print() const; };

options.A: Modifying a non-`mutable` member variable of the class.
options.B: Calling other `const` member functions of the same class.
options.C: Returning a non-`const` reference to a member variable.
options.D: Accessing (reading) non-`mutable` member variables.

text: You have `std::vector<std::string> source = {"a", "b"};` and `std::vector<std::string> dest;`. What is the effect of `dest = std::move(source);`?

options.A: The contents of `source` are copied to `dest`, and `source` remains unchanged.
options.B: `dest` will contain {"a", "b"}.
options.C: `source` will be in a valid but unspecified state, typically empty.
options.D: This operation performs a deep copy of all string data.

text: Which properties are characteristic of a valid Binary Search Tree (BST)?

options.A: For every node, all keys in its left subtree are less than the node's key.
options.B: For every node, all keys in its right subtree are greater than the node's key.
options.C: All leaf nodes are at the same depth.
options.D: Duplicate keys are typically not allowed, or handled with specific rules.

text: When performing a Depth-First Search (DFS) on a graph, how can cycles be detected?

options.A: In an undirected graph, if DFS encounters an already visited node that is not its immediate parent.
options.B: In a directed graph, if DFS encounters a node that is currently in the recursion stack (i.e., being visited).
options.C: By checking if the number of edges is greater than (number of vertices - 1).
options.D: By using a queue to track visited nodes and their parents.

text: For keys in `std::map<Key, Value>`, which type is generally more efficient and safer to use?

options.A: `char*` pointers, as they are lightweight.
options.B: `std::string`, as it handles memory management and comparison correctly.
options.C: `const char*` literals, as they are stored in read-only memory.
options.D: Custom struct with raw `char[]` and manual comparison.

text: You are implementing a generic `Node` for a linked list that can store any data type. Which C++ feature is most appropriate?

options.A: Inheritance with a base `Node` class and derived `IntNode`, `StringNode` classes.
options.B: Using `void*` to store data and `reinterpret_cast` when accessing.
options.C: Class templates, e.g., `template <typename T> class Node { T data; Node<T>* next; };`
options.D: Macros to generate different `Node` types for each data type.

text: How does Resource Acquisition Is Initialization (RAII) apply to custom data structures in C++?

options.A: It ensures that resources (like dynamically allocated memory) are acquired in the constructor.
options.B: It guarantees that acquired resources are automatically released in the destructor.
options.C: It is primarily used for file handles and mutexes, not memory management in data structures.
options.D: It helps prevent memory leaks and ensures exception safety when managing resources.

text: Consider a `std::vector<int> v;`. Which operations could lead to undefined behavior or logical errors if not handled carefully?

options.A: `v[0]` when `v` is empty.
options.B: `v.at(v.size())` when `v` is not empty.
options.C: `v.pop_back()` when `v` is empty.
options.D: `v.reserve(10); v.resize(5);`

text: A recursive DFS algorithm is implemented on a very deep graph. What is the most likely issue if the graph has millions of nodes and a long path?

options.A: Heap overflow due to excessive memory allocation for visited nodes.
options.B: Stack overflow due to deep recursion.
options.C: Integer overflow if node IDs are used as counters.
options.D: Cache miss penalties due to non-local memory access.

text: When defining a struct for a tree node, how might the order of member variables affect memory usage or performance?
code: struct Node { char c; int i; bool b; Node* next; };

options.A: The order of members has no impact on memory usage, only on readability.
options.B: Padding bytes might be inserted by the compiler for alignment, increasing total size.
options.C: Reordering members can reduce the total size of the struct by minimizing padding.
options.D: Accessing misaligned data is always slower on all architectures.
